final_answer: Passive diffusion (passive membrane permeation). The small hydrophobic payload, once localized at the hepatic cell surface via nanobody-receptor binding, spontaneously partitions through the lipid bilayer without requiring receptor-induced endocytosis.

trajectory.thought: I need to analyze this drug delivery problem step by step. The key elements are: (1) payload is a small, non-bioactive, hydrophobic molecule; (2) it's conjugated to a nanobody targeting an orphan receptor on hepatic cells; (3) the PI says it doesn't matter if the receptor doesn't canonically induce endocytosis. I need to identify what cellular process allows the delivery to work even without receptor-mediated endocytosis. The emphasis on the hydrophobicity of the payload is a critical clue — small hydrophobic molecules can passively permeate lipid bilayers. The nanobody serves as a targeting vehicle to localize the hydrophobic payload at the cell surface (via receptor binding), and from there the inherent hydrophobicity drives membrane permeation. This doesn't require the receptor to trigger endocytosis, which is exactly what the PI is saying. The cellular/biophysical process is passive diffusion through the lipid bilayer.

trajectory.thought: The PI says identification of the orphan receptor's canonical function doesn't matter, even if it doesn't induce endocytosis. The critical clue is that the payload is a small, NON-BIOACTIVE, HYDROPHOBIC molecule. The nanobody binds the orphan receptor at the hepatic cell surface, localizing the hydrophobic payload directly at the plasma membrane (a lipid bilayer). Small hydrophobic molecules have high lipid-water partition coefficients and can passively permeate through the lipid bilayer by passive diffusion — completely independent of whether the receptor triggers endocytosis. The receptor binding via the nanobody serves only as a TARGETING mechanism to concentrate the hydrophobic payload at the membrane, after which passive membrane permeation takes over. This is the process the PI is counting on.
